Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I'm juggling so much at 27 with limited resources. I'd carve out about 30 hours a week, but it wasn't in neat blocks - more like grabbing time whenever I could between work and life responsibilities. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often print things out or work through problems physically rather than just reading on screen. The routine existed, but it was flexible out of necessity rather than choice.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I couldn't afford the luxury of a perfectly regular schedule - some weeks I'd cram everything into a few intense days, other weeks I'd spread it out more evenly. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied most intensely. Being 27 and managing limited resources means I have to be adaptable rather than rigid about timing.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment requirements or see if there were any announcements I'd missed. Sometimes my peer support group would message me about something posted, and that would prompt me to check in. Honestly, with my attendance being what it is, I relied on the LMS to catch up on what I missed in person. It wasn't a daily habit, more like when I specifically needed something.
Q4: I wouldn't say the LMS ever became truly routine for me - it was more of a tool I used when necessary. The peer support intervention actually helped because my study partners would remind me to check certain things. There were brief periods where I'd log in more regularly, but those habits didn't really stick. My engagement level stayed pretty low because I found other ways to learn that worked better for my kinesthetic style.
Q5: The discussion posts and peer interaction features were actually really valuable to me because connecting with other students helped me understand concepts better. When I could engage with peers about the material, it felt more like the hands-on learning I prefer. The assignment submission system was essential too, since my assignment scores were strong even when other metrics weren't. Videos were useful but only when I could actively work alongside them.
Q6: I often skipped or minimally used the video lectures because just watching passively doesn't work well for my learning style. I'd watch maybe 40 minutes worth and then need to do something more active with the material. Some of the reading resources I'd download once and never return to in the LMS because I was working with printed copies instead. If something felt like passive consumption, I'd find ways around it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually do something with my hands or engage actively with the material. Discussion posts felt worthwhile because I was constructing my own thoughts and responding to others. Anything that let me practice or apply concepts rather than just absorb information passively was valuable. If I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams, that helped justify the time investment too.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford given my time constraints and low resources. Required activities got priority because they directly impacted my grade, which I needed to maintain. That said, when optional activities involved peer interaction or hands-on practice, I was more likely to engage with them. The peer support aspect of the course made some optional collaborative activities feel more appealing than they otherwise might have.
Q9: Absolutely - most of my real studying happened offline with printed materials, handwritten notes, and physical practice problems. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them with a pen and paper because that's how I actually learn. I also used some external YouTube videos and free resources online when the course materials weren't clicking for me. The LMS was just the starting point; the real learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: So many situations - I'd spend hours working through problems by hand at my kitchen table with printed materials, and none of that showed up anywhere. When I met with my peer support group in person to discuss concepts and quiz each other, that was invisible to the system. I'd re-read my handwritten notes on the bus or before bed. Some of my most productive study sessions involved no technology at all.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a PDF or resource once, download or print it, and then that material lived in my physical study space. I might never click on that resource again in the LMS, but I'd have reviewed it multiple times on paper. My assignment scores being so high while my task completion looks low is partly explained by this - I was engaging deeply, just not digitally.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when my anxiety would spike or when money was tight and I was stressed about resources. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit me hard and made it difficult to bounce back into engagement mode. Sometimes the workload across all six online courses felt overwhelming, and I'd have to triage what got my attention. Those were the times my already-low attendance would drop even further.
Q13: Definitely - when I was fatigued or overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing to go because I'd default to my offline study methods instead. Competing responsibilities meant I couldn't always engage with the platform the way it was designed to be used. Confusion about course structure sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to deal with figuring things out. My low engagement level reflects that I was often too stretched thin to use the system fully.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things. With moderate anxiety, sometimes I'd log in intending to study but end up just clicking around without really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine learning. I'd log the time but not really be present mentally.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I wasn't sure how to approach them. My low resilience means I tend to avoid things that feel difficult rather than pushing through immediately. Sometimes I knew an activity was important but my motivation just wasn't there - that index of 3 out of 5 is accurate. The peer support helped with this because having accountability partners pushed me to do things I might otherwise delay.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS usage - I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached. My assignment completion rate was low overall, but when I did complete things, it was usually deadline-driven. The pressure of a deadline could temporarily override my low motivation and push me to engage. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged with the LMS even less than I did.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback on my assignments - which happened often given my high assignment scores - it encouraged me to keep putting effort into that work. But if feedback was delayed or critical, my low resilience made it hard to bounce back quickly. The peer support intervention meant I also got informal feedback from classmates, which sometimes felt more motivating than official instructor feedback.
Q18: The course structure with six online courses simultaneously made it hard to give each one the attention it deserved. Having peer support built in was helpful because it gave me a reason to engage beyond just my own motivation. The structure assumed more consistent online engagement than I could realistically provide given my learning style and circumstances. I adapted by finding my own ways to meet the learning objectives even if my participation looked different than expected.
Q19: The kinesthetic, hands-on aspects of any course made engagement much easier for me - when I could actually do something rather than just read or watch. The peer interaction features helped because connecting with others made the learning feel more active. What made it harder was when everything was designed for passive consumption or when the platform wasn't intuitive. Having limited resources also meant I couldn't always access things the way they were intended.
Q20: I think students might be searching for understanding without finding it, clicking through materials trying to make sense of things but not feeling confident enough to submit work. Or they could be dealing with anxiety or perfectionism that makes them engage with content but freeze up when it comes to actually turning something in. Sometimes the LMS activity is about trying to figure out what's expected rather than actually learning. Life circumstances might also mean they're interrupted constantly while trying to work.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience - my LMS activity was low but I earned a Merit and scored nearly 80 on exams with almost perfect assignment scores. I was learning effectively through offline methods that didn't show up in the system. The logs don't capture studying from printed materials, peer discussions outside the platform, or hands-on practice. Performance and visible engagement aren't the same thing at all.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone accesses the platform, and maybe patterns around deadlines. They capture digital footprints well. But they completely miss offline learning, the quality of engagement during logged sessions, and whether someone is actually understanding the material. They can't measure if I'm fully focused or anxiously scrolling, and they don't see the hours I spend with printed notes.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, trying to understand and apply it, not just passively receiving information. For me, it involves doing something physical - writing, practicing, discussing with peers. It's about genuine curiosity and effort toward learning, even when motivation is low. Engagement isn't about how many times I log in; it's about whether I'm actually growing in my understanding and skills.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I rely on peer support and in-person study sessions to stay on track. It wouldn't capture the hours I spend with printed materials and handwritten notes, which is where most of my real learning happens. My emotional state - the anxiety, the low resilience, the effort it takes to keep going when things get hard - none of that appears in click data. The system sees a disengaged student when really I'm just engaged in ways that don't register digitally.
